---
title: "Spatial and Graphical Bayesian Models: Conditional Independence, GMRFs, and Structured Priors"
---

## Learning Outcomes

After this chapter, you should be able to:

- read model structure through conditional independence statements;
- factorize a joint distribution from a directed acyclic graph;
- explain why sparse precision matrices encode local dependence in Gaussian Markov random fields;
- describe CAR-style spatial priors conceptually;
- explain message passing at a high level.

## 1. Structured Dependence

Many Bayesian models are best understood not through formulas alone but through the graph of conditional dependencies they imply. Graphical models give a language for:

- sparse factorization;
- local Markov structure;
- efficient computation;
- interpretable dependence assumptions.

This is especially useful in spatial models, multilevel systems, and latent-state models [@murphy2023].

## 2. Directed Graphical Models

If a directed acyclic graph (DAG) has nodes $X_1,\dots,X_p$, then the joint density factorizes as

$$
p(x_1,\dots,x_p)=\prod_{j=1}^p p(x_j \mid \operatorname{pa}(x_j)),
$$

where $\operatorname{pa}(x_j)$ denotes the parents of node $j$.

This factorization is not only computational. It is a modeling claim about which variables depend directly on which others.

## 3. Bayesian Networks

A Bayesian network is a DAG plus conditional probability models at each node.

### Example

If

$$
X \to Z \leftarrow Y
$$

then

$$
p(x,y,z)=p(x)p(y)p(z \mid x,y).
$$

This implies $X \perp\!\!\!\perp Y$ marginally, but conditioning on $Z$ generally introduces dependence. Graphical reasoning therefore helps avoid conditioning mistakes that are easy to miss in raw algebra.

## 4. Gaussian Markov Random Fields

For a multivariate Gaussian vector

$$
x \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,Q^{-1}),
$$

the precision matrix $Q$ encodes conditional independence:

$$
Q_{ij}=0
\quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad
x_i \perp\!\!\!\perp x_j \mid x_{-(i,j)}.
$$

This is one of the central structural facts behind Gaussian Markov random fields.

### Why precision, not covariance

- sparse covariance does not generally encode local Markov structure;
- sparse precision does.

This is why GMRFs are computationally attractive: sparse precision matrices can be manipulated much more efficiently in large structured models.

## 5. Conditional Autoregressive Priors

Spatial random effects on neighboring regions are often modeled through CAR structure. At a high level, the conditional distribution of a region effect $\phi_i$ depends on neighboring region effects:

$$
\phi_i \mid \phi_{-i}
\sim
\mathcal{N}\!\left(
\frac{\sum_{j \sim i} w_{ij}\phi_j}{\sum_{j \sim i} w_{ij}},
\frac{\tau^2}{\sum_{j \sim i} w_{ij}}
\right).
$$

This says each region is shrunk toward a weighted average of its neighbors.

### Conceptual interpretation

CAR priors encode local smoothing. Nearby regions are encouraged, not forced, to look similar.

### Caution

Intrinsic CAR models can be improper and often require constraints or careful identifiability handling.

## 6. SAR And Related Spatial Structures

Spatial autoregressive ideas can also be written in simultaneous form, where dependence appears through a transformed regression or latent-error structure. The key statistical issue is the same: spatial correlation is structured by a neighborhood graph or weight matrix, not by arbitrary pairwise dependence.

## 7. Message Passing Overview

When a graph factorizes nicely, local computations can be propagated across the graph. In tree-like or chain-like structures, this becomes message passing:

- forward-backward in HMMs;
- belief propagation in trees;
- junction-tree methods in decomposable graphs.

The central idea is that local conditional independence allows global inference to be decomposed into repeated local updates.

## 8. Spatial And Graphical Bayesian Workflow

In practice, the hard part is rarely writing a symbolic factorization. It is deciding whether the graph itself is scientifically defensible:

- what counts as a neighbor;
- which edges are substantively plausible;
- whether smoothing should be local or global;
- whether conditional independence assumptions are too strong.

Graphical structure is therefore part of model specification, not merely a computational trick.

## 9. Diagnostics And Pitfalls

### Confusing sparse precision with sparse covariance

These encode different notions of dependence.

### Using neighborhood matrices mechanically

Spatial adjacency is a modeling decision, not a default truth.

### Ignoring impropriety in intrinsic spatial priors

Some structured priors need constraints for identifiability.

### Treating the graph as purely computational

Every edge or missing edge is a substantive claim.

## 10. Exercises

1. Factorize a joint density from a simple DAG and identify at least one conditional independence relation.
2. Explain why zeros in a Gaussian precision matrix imply conditional independence.
3. Describe the prior meaning of a CAR spatial effect.
4. Compare a spatial smoothing prior with an exchangeable random-effects prior.
5. Explain at a high level why message passing is possible in sparse graphical models.

## 11. Summary

Spatial and graphical Bayesian models are unified by conditional independence:

- DAGs encode directed factorization;
- GMRFs encode local dependence through sparse precision;
- CAR priors express spatial smoothing through neighborhood structure;
- message passing exploits graph structure for inference.

## References

Key references are @gelman2013 and @murphy2023.
